# Quantum Optimal Transport — Hands-On Course

> Machine index for AI assistants. Each entry: notebook path — title — purpose.
> Generated by scripts/gen_llms_txt.py (do not hand-edit). See AGENTS.md for how to tutor.

## 00_GettingStarted
- [notebooks/00_GettingStarted/01_environment_setup.ipynb](notebooks/00_GettingStarted/01_environment_setup.ipynb) — 01 — Environment Setup — Confirm your tools are installed and run your very first quantum circuit, so everything that follows runs cleanly.
- [notebooks/00_GettingStarted/02_what_is_optimal_transport.ipynb](notebooks/00_GettingStarted/02_what_is_optimal_transport.ipynb) — 02 — What Is Optimal Transport? — Build the central idea of the whole course by hand: moving a pile of mass into a new shape at the least possible cost.
## 01_QuantumFoundations
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/01_amplitudes_and_superposition.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/01_amplitudes_and_superposition.ipynb) — 01 — Amplitudes & Superposition — Write a qubit as a pair of complex amplitudes and meet superposition — the first genuinely quantum idea.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/02_the_bloch_sphere.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/02_the_bloch_sphere.ipynb) — 02 — The Bloch Sphere — Turn every pure qubit into a single point on a sphere — the compass we navigate by for the rest of the course.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/03_measurement_and_born_rule.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/03_measurement_and_born_rule.ipynb) — 03 — Measurement & the Born Rule — Turn amplitudes into the probabilities a measurement actually returns — and discover the one thing a measurement cannot see.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/04_shot_noise_and_first_circuit.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/04_shot_noise_and_first_circuit.ipynb) — 04 — Shot Noise & Your First Circuit — Close the qubit arc: sample the Born rule the way a real device does, watch the estimate converge, and run it all as a genuine Qiskit circuit.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/05_from_states_to_density_matrix.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/05_from_states_to_density_matrix.ipynb) — 05 — From States to the Density Matrix — Promote a state vector to an operator — the density matrix $\rho$ — and meet the central character of the rest of the course.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/06_pure_vs_mixed.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/06_pure_vs_mixed.ipynb) — 06 — Pure vs Mixed — Meet the maximally mixed state, set it beside $|+\rangle$, and find the single contrast the whole course is built to explain.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/07_fidelity_and_trace_distance.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/07_fidelity_and_trace_distance.ipynb) — 07 — Fidelity & Trace Distance — Pick up two honest yardsticks for "how different are two states?", and watch mixedness become a position inside the Bloch ball.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/08_tomography_of_a_noisy_qubit.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/08_tomography_of_a_noisy_qubit.ipynb) — 08 — Tomography of a Noisy Qubit — Close the density arc: reconstruct $\rho$ from real, noisy measurements and watch purity fall below $1$ — the honest reason the density matrix exists.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/09_tensor_and_partial_trace.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/09_tensor_and_partial_trace.ipynb) — 09 — Tensor Product & Partial Trace — Combine two qubits into one system, then recover a part — meeting the quantum version of a marginal.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/10_entanglement.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/10_entanglement.ipynb) — 10 — Entanglement — Build a two-qubit state that is perfectly definite as a whole, yet whose parts are maximally uncertain — something no classical joint distribution can do.
- [notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/11_quantum_channels.ipynb](notebooks/01_QuantumFoundations/11_quantum_channels.ipynb) — 11 — Quantum Channels — How a quantum state *changes* — and how it picks up noise. Meet CPTP maps, watch a channel mix $|+\rangle$ toward $I/2$, and close Movement 1 of the course.
## 02_InformationAndGeometry
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/01_shannon_entropy.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/01_shannon_entropy.ipynb) — 01 — Shannon Entropy — Measure uncertainty as average surprise — the first tool of information theory, and the spine of everything that follows.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/02_kl_divergence.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/02_kl_divergence.ipynb) — 02 — KL Divergence — Measure the cost of a wrong model — the "extra bits" of using $q$ when the truth is $p$. This is the parent quantity of the entire course.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/03_mutual_information.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/03_mutual_information.ipynb) — 03 — Mutual Information — Measure how much two variables share — the information one carries about the other.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/04_transfer_entropy.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/04_transfer_entropy.ipynb) — 04 — Transfer Entropy — Close the classical-information arc: make information flow **directed** — who drives whom — and meet the honesty caveats every estimate needs.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/05_the_probability_simplex.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/05_the_probability_simplex.ipynb) — 05 — The Probability Simplex — See the space of *all* categorical distributions as a single geometric object — a triangle — and ask the question that opens the geometry arc: what is the natural way to measure distance on it?
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/06_fisher_rao_metric.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/06_fisher_rao_metric.ipynb) — 06 — The Fisher–Rao Metric — Discover that the simplex is curved: the natural metric of statistical distinguishability blows up at the corners, so the boundary is infinitely far away.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/07_fisher_rao_geodesics.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/07_fisher_rao_geodesics.ipynb) — 07 — Fisher–Rao Geodesics — Compute the geodesic distance on the curved simplex and draw the shortest paths — great-circle arcs that bow away from the boundary.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/08_two_geometries_one_simplex.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/08_two_geometries_one_simplex.ipynb) — 08 — Two Geometries on One Simplex — Close the geometry arc with the punchline that powers the rest of the course: the same simplex carries *two* distinct geometries — information and transport — and they give different answers to "what is the halfway distribution?"
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/09_umegaki_relative_entropy.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/09_umegaki_relative_entropy.ipynb) — 09 — Umegaki Relative Entropy — Lift the KL divergence to density matrices — and watch it see the coherence a classical KL is blind to.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/10_quantum_mutual_information.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/10_quantum_mutual_information.ipynb) — 10 — Quantum Mutual Information — Lift mutual information to density matrices — and find a Bell pair shares twice what any classical pair can.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/11_negative_conditional_entropy.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/11_negative_conditional_entropy.ipynb) — 11 — Negative Conditional Entropy — Meet the sharpest signature of entanglement: a conditional entropy that drops below zero — impossible for any classical pair.
- [notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/12_bures_distance.ipynb](notebooks/02_InformationAndGeometry/12_bures_distance.ipynb) — 12 — Bures Distance — Close Movement 2: lift the Fisher–Rao distance to density matrices, and complete the classical-to-quantum information dictionary.
## 03_ClassicalOptimalTransport
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/01_the_monge_problem.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/01_the_monge_problem.ipynb) — 01 — The Monge Problem — Ask optimal transport's founding question — *what is the cheapest way to reshape one pile of mass into another?* — and meet Monge's original answer: a deterministic map.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/02_where_monge_breaks.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/02_where_monge_breaks.ipynb) — 02 — Where Monge Breaks — Meet the obstruction that defeats Monge's deterministic map: when one pile of mass must *split* to fill another, no function can describe the move.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/03_the_kantorovich_lp.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/03_the_kantorovich_lp.ipynb) — 03 — The Kantorovich LP — Relax Monge's map into a *coupling* that may split mass, and optimal transport becomes a linear program that always has a solution.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/04_birkhoff_and_assignment.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/04_birkhoff_and_assignment.ipynb) — 04 — Birkhoff and the Assignment Problem — Close the Monge→Kantorovich arc with its most elegant special case — uniform marginals, where transport becomes the classical assignment problem — and open the transport row of the classical↔quantum dictionary.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/05_wasserstein_distance.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/05_wasserstein_distance.ipynb) — 05 — The Wasserstein Distance — Promote the Kantorovich *cost* into a genuine *distance* — one that, unlike the KL divergence, listens to the geometry of the ground space.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/06_1d_quantile_closed_form.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/06_1d_quantile_closed_form.ipynb) — 06 — The 1-D Closed Form — On the line, the whole linear program collapses to *sort and match by quantile* — and $W_1$ becomes the plain area between two cumulative curves.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/07_mccann_geodesic.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/07_mccann_geodesic.ipynb) — 07 — The McCann Geodesic — Close the Wasserstein arc with its most beautiful feature: the $W_2$ geodesic *slides* mass through the ground space — the transport-geometry half of the two-geometries hinge.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/08_kantorovich_duality.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/08_kantorovich_duality.ipynb) — 08 — Kantorovich Duality — Every transport LP has a hidden twin: a *dual* that prices positions instead of moving mass. The two views give the same number — and the dual is where the deep structure lives.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/09_entropic_regularization.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/09_entropic_regularization.ipynb) — 09 — Entropic Regularization — Reward the transport plan a little for being *spread out*, and the problem turns smooth, strongly convex, and fast. At its heart sits one object: the Gibbs kernel.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/10_sinkhorn_algorithm.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/10_sinkhorn_algorithm.ipynb) — 10 — The Sinkhorn Algorithm — Bend the Gibbs kernel onto the marginal constraints by nothing more than rescaling rows and columns in turn. Five lines, two matrix products per step — the algorithm that made optimal transport fast.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/11_amari_bridge.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/11_amari_bridge.ipynb) — 11 — Amari's Bridge — The identity the whole course has been walking toward: the Sinkhorn plan is the **KL projection** of the Gibbs kernel onto the transportation polytope. Transport geometry *is* information geometry.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/12_bures_wasserstein.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/12_bures_wasserstein.ipynb) — 12 — Bures–Wasserstein — For one special family — the Gaussians — optimal transport needs no algorithm at all. $W_2$ is a closed-form expression in the means and covariances.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/13_the_bures_bridge.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/13_the_bures_bridge.ipynb) — 13 — The Bures Bridge — Watch one formula change identity. The matrix term of the Gaussian Wasserstein distance, fed density matrices instead of covariances, *becomes* the quantum Bures distance of `02/12`. This is the door into quantum optimal transport.
- [notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/14_benamou_brenier_otto.ipynb](notebooks/03_ClassicalOptimalTransport/14_benamou_brenier_otto.ipynb) — 14 — Benamou–Brenier and Otto — Close Module 3 by viewing the $W_2$ geodesic two ways — McCann's explicit affine map, and Benamou–Brenier's least-energy flow — and complete the dictionary that carries transport into the quantum world.
## 04_QuantumOptimalTransport
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/01_why_qot.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/01_why_qot.ipynb) — Session 12 — Why Quantum Optimal Transport? — S12 opens **Movement 4 (Quantum Optimal Transport)** by answering the question that has
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/02_sdp.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/02_sdp.ipynb) — Session 13 — Coupling QOT = a Semidefinite Program — S13 promises and delivers a concrete computational definition of quantum optimal
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/03_quantum_sinkhorn.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/03_quantum_sinkhorn.ipynb) — Session 14 — Quantum Sinkhorn — S14 closes the M2 ↔ M3 ↔ M4 loop. We:
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/04_capstone.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/04_capstone.ipynb) — Session 15 — Capstone: Coupling Measures on a Kuramoto Dyad — Assemble everything into a real, *unresolved* question:
- [notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/05_frontier_synthesis.ipynb](notebooks/04_QuantumOptimalTransport/05_frontier_synthesis.ipynb) — Session 16 — Frontier & Synthesis — The final session. We:
